previewTitle.value: Inspiring Action to Save the Ocean

title.value: Inspiring Action to Save the Ocean

text.value: Cristina Mittermeier is a pioneer of conservation photography. Now, with the support of Rolex’s Perpetual Planet Initiative, she is continuing to take photographs that spark conversations around the ocean and inspire action to protect it.

Cristina Mittermeier may have grown up in the mountains of Central Mexico, but for much of her life, she has championed the ocean. Today, Mittermeier is one of the world’s most influential conservation photographers. She uses stunning visual storytelling to advocate for the environment, with a focus on the ocean, and to encourage conversations about the threats that plague the marine world.

Mittermeier’s fascination with the ocean started with pictures of marine life in books and took her all the way to the Monterrey Institute of Technology and Higher Education, where she studied Biochemical Engineering in Marine Sciences before going on to work as a marine biologist. However, through her travels, she quickly realized that science alone was not enough for her. The research was not reaching the public as she had hoped or spurring the kind of action that would safeguard the seas and the people who depend on them.

Mittermeier began teaching herself how to use a camera and attended the Fine Art Photography programme at the Corcoran College for the Arts in Washington, D.C., where she learned to craft striking visuals. She spent years photographing terrestrial subjects and people, until she met her partner, Canadian photographer Paul Nicklen. As a well-established marine photographer, he was able to help her begin her journey to taking some of the world’s most iconic underwater photographs.
I left biology in the hope of becoming a voice for the ocean and its peoples. To tell its story with a camera.

Mittermeier has always been clear about one thing: she was not interested in photography for the sake of capturing brilliant pictures alone. Instead, she wanted to use her visual storytelling to spark real conversations and real action. In fact, Mittermeier is a pioneer of conservation photography, and even coined the term. In 2005, she created the International League of Conservation Photographers (ILCP) – the first organization of its kind – to help photographers and filmmakers from around the world drive actual conservation efforts through ethical storytelling.

Mittermeier herself has travelled to more than 130 countries and her photographs have appeared in a wide range of acclaimed media outlets, including National Geographic, TIME, The New York Times, The Washington Post, CNN and Bloomberg TV. She has brought millions of people closer to the ocean by capturing scenes such as schools of spotted eagle rays in synchronous dances and giant sperm whales diving kilometres deep to feed.

Her stories show how human well-being is closely intertwined with ocean health. This work has helped unmask the extent and urgency of the threats that the ocean faces, from plastic pollution to climate change, and how these affect the lives of animals and people who live at the edge of the seas. One photograph she took of a starving polar bear dragging itself on the barren arctic tundra in 2017, for instance, went viral, triggering conversations about the effects of climate change on the polar world.

Mittermeier has received numerous awards for her work, including the Smithsonian Conservation Photographer of the Year Award, the Mission Award from the North American Nature Photography Association and the Imaging Award for Photographers Who Give Back. In 2018, she was named one of National Geographic’s Adventurers of the Year. In 2023 alone, she has received the Photographic Achievement Award from the International Photography Council, been recognized by the WINGS Women of Discovery Awards for her significant contributions to world knowledge and science through exploration, and shared the Lucie Foundation Humanitarian Award in Photography with Paul Nicklen. She is also a Sony Artisan of Imagery.

In 2021, Mittermeier was awarded the Seattle Aquarium Sylvia Earle Medal in recognition of her three decades of courageous journalism. She is also the first female photographer to reach one million followers on Instagram, where she shares captivating images of wildlife and local communities, along with her personal stories, to nudge people closer to conservationism.
Today, with renewed purpose, I am hoping to do my part to help ensure that future generations can experience the wonder and beauty of the ocean, just as I have.

Throughout her life, Mittermeier has strived to build communities of ocean advocates. She mentors young female photographers and works with local communities to help them become voices of ocean conservation.

In 2014, along with Nicklen, she co-founded a non-profit organization called SeaLegacy, a global marketing, education and communication agency for the ocean. At the nexus of climate action and sustainable solutions, SeaLegacy creates the strategies and content that move audiences into action by working at the intersection of art, science, and conservation. So far, SeaLegacy has been part of some 45 expeditions that have captured over 7 million images.

Mittermeier has published research in several scientific journals and co-authored and edited 27 books on conservation issues. She is also a sought-after speaker at international conservation conferences and was part of the UN Ocean Conference in Lisbon in 2022, as well as the Convention on Biological Diversity.

Mittermeier has been part of several conservation efforts, including campaigns to expand the Marine Protected Area in the Galápagos along with the banning of gill-net fishing off the coast of California. The Perpetual Planet Initiative supports the multi-award-winning photographer on her mission to protect the future of the ocean.

metadescription.value: Rolex supports exploration as a means to preserve the natural world. Discover more about Rolex's commitment to conservation through the Perpetual Planet initiative.

previewTitle.value: Master photographer on a mission to help us see the sea - Environment

pageTitle.value: 2017 Rolex National Geographic Explorer of the Year - Brian Skerry

pageCaption.value: Renowned photojournalist Brian Skerry has spent more than 10,000 hours underwater over the course of his 30-year career. Growing up in Massachusetts, he was drawn to the ocean and all its mystery, never imagining that one day he would land his dream job and that it would be underwater. With discipline and hard work, he became a contributing photographer for National Geographic in 1998 and through his stories shed light on both the beauty and the fragility of the ocean and its inhabitants.

metadescription.value: Through his stories, renowned photojournalist Brian Skerry is shedding light on both the beauty and the fragility of the ocean. Learn more on rolex.org.
